summary: Drew DeVault excoriates Apollo GraphQL for switching Apollo Federation 2 from the MIT license to the non-free Elastic License v2 while claiming the change benefits the open source community. He systematically deconstructs Apollo's announcement, showing how the new license strips users of sublicensing, resale, and hosted service rights while granting Apollo exclusive commercial exploitation. DeVault places this move in a broader pattern of companies building products on open source community contributions, then pulling a bait-and-switch to a restrictive license. He calls out the CEOs behind similar moves at Elastic, MongoDB, and Confluent, and warns developers against signing CLAs that enable such exploitation.
key_points: The Elastic License v2 removes critical freedoms including sublicensing, resale, modification of license key code, and use in hosted services — making it definitively not open source by the OSD
Apollo's announcement uses deliberately misleading language, claiming to 'protect the community' while actually securing exclusive commercial rights for the corporation
This follows a recurring pattern: build open source, leverage community contributions to grow the product and market, then switch to a restrictive license and capture all the value
634 contributors gave code under the open source social contract, and Apollo unilaterally changed the terms — a betrayal DeVault calls 'shitting in the pool of open source'
CLAs that require copyright assignment are the mechanism enabling these bait-and-switch moves; developers should retain their copyright and use the DCO instead
money_quotes: Gather around, my friends, for there is another company which thinks we are stupid and we enjoy having our faces spat in.
The new license gives Apollo exclusive right to commercial exploitation of the software — so they can 'exploit our work', but the community itself cannot.
Free and open source software can be commercialized — this is an essential requirement of the philosophy! However, it cannot be exclusively commercialized.

Businesses which participate in the FOSS ecosystem must give up their intellectual property monopoly, and allow the commercial ecosystem to flourish within their community — not just within their own ledger.

The community built their projects on top of Apollo based on the open source social contract, and gave their time, effort, and copyright for their contributions to it, and Apollo pulled the rug out from under them.

This wave of nonfree commercial gaslighting is part of a pattern where a company builds an open-source product, leverages the open-source community to build a market for it and to directly improve the product via their contributions, then switches to a nonfree license and steals the work for themselves, fucking everyone else over.
They are morally bankrupt assholes and rent-seekers who gaslight and exploit the open source community for personal gain.
Retain your copyright over your contributions and contribute to projects which are collectively owned by their community — because that's how you honor your community.
key_insight: Companies that switch from open source to restrictive licenses after building on community contributions are executing a deliberate bait-and-switch that exploits the open source social contract, and CLAs are the mechanism that enables it.
